figures.caption: FIG. 1: Sensor configurations in the fluid domain. Circles, squares, and crosses denote horizontal velocity, u, wall-normal velocity, v, and temperature, θ′, sensors, respectively. The background shows an instantaneous DNS temperature field at R = 120, Pr = 10.
FIG. 2: Time evolution of amplitude coefficients: comparison between DNS projection, ci, and KF estimates, ĉi for the six modes with the largest mean energy, ⟨c2j ⟩.
FIG. 3: Time evolution of estimation, ec, velocity, eu, and temperature, eθ, errors.
FIG. 4: Time evolution of amplitude coefficients: comparison between DNS projection, ci, and KF estimates, ĉi for the six modes with the largest mean energy, ⟨c2j ⟩.
FIG. 5: Time evolution of amplitude coefficients: comparison between DNS projection, ci, and KF estimates, ĉi for the six modes with the largest mean energy, ⟨c2j ⟩.
FIG. 6: Time evolution of the dominant ROM coefficients for the velocity-only observation strategy at R = 120, Pr = 10: DNS projection ci (black) and KF estimates ĉi (blue).
FIG. 7: Time evolution of filter, velocity and temperature errors for the velocity-only observation strategy at R = 120, Pr = 10.

FIG. 8: Greedy sensor removal results at R = 120, Pr = 10, η = 1. (a) Evolution of filter error and sensor count during the removal process. (b, c) Resulting probe configurations for the coarse grid and greedy strategies at m = 12. Circles, squares, and crosses denote horizontal velocity, wall-normal velocity, and temperature sensors, respectively. The background shows an instantaneous DNS temperature field.
FIG. 9: Coarse grid strategy with m = 12: time evolution of the six most energetic amplitude coefficients from DNS projection, ci, and KF estimates, ĉi.
FIG. 10: Greedy strategy with m = 12: time evolution of the six most energetic amplitude coefficients from DNS projection, ci, and KF estimates, ĉi.

FIG. 11: Error metrics Eu, Eθ, and Ec for the greedy strategy (m = 12) across four DNS realisations with different random seeds η. Error bars represent the mean standard deviation of the error signals.

FIG. 12: Greedy sensor configuration at m = 12 overlaid on the root-mean-square horizontal velocity urms for four DNS realizations with different initial conditions. Symbols indicate the retained probe locations.

FIG. 13: Time evolution of the most energetic modes for the chaotic case (R = 120, Pr = 10, η = 1, m = 48): DNS projection ci (black solid lines), baseline isotropic filter with Q = βI, R = I (blue dashed lines), and filter with data-estimated covariances, Q̂, R̂ (red dash-dot lines).

FIG. 14: Sensitivity analysis for the chaotic case R = 120, Pr = 10. (a) Influence of the model-to-measurement noise ratio β. (b) Influence of the ROM dimension n and the observation dimension m. Error bars represent the mean standard deviation of the error signals.

FIG. 15: Instantaneous temperature field, θ: (a) DNS, (b) Kalman filter reconstruction with n = 96 ROM modes and m = 48 observations, (c) Kalman filter reconstruction with n = 192 ROM modes and m = 192 observations. Results are shown for Ra/Rac = 120 and Pr = 10.
FIG. 16: Correlation matrices of the estimated model-error, Q̂, and measurement-error, R̂, covariances, for the chaotic case R = 120, Pr = 10, η = 1, with the m = 48 coarse grid.
